title: file write/edit/notebook_edit: canonicalize races against fs::write (symlink swap TOCTOU)
description:
Steps to reproduce:
1. 

Expected: 
Actual: 

**Dimension:** Security and Access (TOCTOU)

**Location:** src/tools/file/write.rs:20-34,62; src/tools/file/edit.rs:20-40,98; src/tools/file/notebook.rs:77-85,198.

**Violation:** Each write/edit/notebook_edit canonicalize()s, then later calls fs::write on a path-based re-open. Between the canonicalize and write, another process can replace the path with a symlink. fs::write follows symlinks on open. The canonicalized path is the inode-at-canonicalize, not inode-at-write. For any attacker with write access to an intermediate directory (/tmp, shared worktrees), this is trivially exploitable. The guardrails::check_file_access gate also operates on the canonicalized string and is bypassable via the same TOCTOU window. edit_file's read-then-write (lines 69, 98) can observe content X and write Y to inode Z.

**Mandated Refactor:**
1. Open ONCE with OpenOptions().read(true).write(true).custom_flags(libc::O_NOFOLLOW) on Unix / FILE_FLAG_OPEN_REPARSE_POINT on Windows. All subsequent reads/writes go through the SAME file handle — no path-based re-open.
2. For write_file new-file case use .create_new(true).custom_flags(O_NOFOLLOW) to refuse a pre-existing symlink.
3. Derive the path-for-logging from fstat(fd) or /proc/self/fd/N (Unix) — not from the user input.
4. Apply guardrails::check_file_access AFTER open, against the fstat-derived path.
